question: An accountant reconciles incoming bank payments against many waiting `order` process instances. The active trigger comes from the bank account, not from Tasklist. What does the routing best-practice recommend for modelling and integration?

options.text: Model an intermediate message catch event in each order process; build a custom search screen so the accountant can correlate a 'Paid' click to the right instance(s) via the Camunda API.
Model a user task per order so the accountant claims and completes it in Tasklist.
Model a multi-instance receive task at process start so a single payment fans out to every order.
Use a non-interrupting boundary signal event so one 'Paid' broadcast updates all open orders at once.

a.text: The 'Handling messages sent by a user' section says to model a message wait point (not a user task) and to build a custom search screen; correlating the user's action to the right instance is done by custom code calling the Camunda API.

b.text: The docs argue against a user task here — the accountant is not waiting on a Tasklist entry; the active trigger is the external payment event in the bank account.

c.text: A multi-instance receive task would block one parent instance until all sub-instances complete, which does not match the fan-in pattern where each order waits independently for its own payment.

d.text: Signals are broadcast and uncorrelated, so a single 'Paid' signal would notify every waiting order, not just the one that was actually paid.

explanation: When the human is not the active trigger (the external bank payment is), the docs recommend modelling the wait point as a message catch event rather than a user task, because the accountant does not want to monitor a queue in Tasklist. A custom screen lets the accountant find waiting orders, and the 'Paid' action becomes a correlated message published through the Camunda API to the right process instance.

docs.title: Routing events to processes - best practices
